parsed.title: Revisiting the Platonic Representation Hypothesis: An Aristotelian View
parsed.venue_hint: ICML or NeurIPS submission (ML conference); arXiv preprint Feb 2026
parsed.abstract: The paper argues that existing metrics used to measure representational similarity are confounded by network scale (width and depth), which inflates similarity scores. The authors propose a permutation-based null-calibration framework that transforms any similarity metric into a calibrated score with statistical guarantees. Applied to the Platonic Representation Hypothesis, the framework shows that apparent convergence under global spectral measures (e.g., CKA) largely disappears after calibration, while local neighborhood similarity retains significant cross-modal agreement. The authors propose the Aristotelian Representation Hypothesis: representations converge to shared local neighborhood relationships rather than global structure.

sections.introduction: The paper introduces representational similarity metrics and the Platonic Representation Hypothesis (PRH) of Huh et al. 2024. It identifies two confounders: (i) model width inflates spectral metrics because expected similarity under independence scales with d/n; (ii) model depth inflates max-aggregated similarity due to the look-elsewhere/multiple comparisons effect. The authors motivate a null-calibration framework based on permutations and preview the finding that global convergence largely disappears after calibration while local neighborhood agreement persists, motivating the Aristotelian Representation Hypothesis.
sections.related_work: Surveys representational similarity metrics (CCA/SVCCA/PWCCA, CKA, RSA, mKNN, Procrustes/shape distances) and prior reliability work (debiased CKA by Murphy et al., Chun et al.; deconfounding by Cui et al.; resampling-based maxT procedures). Notes prior work has not unified bias correction with aggregation-aware calibration in a metric-agnostic way, and reviews the PRH and follow-ups.
sections.method: The authors define a null hypothesis H0 of no relationship beyond marginal statistics, operationalized via random permutations of sample correspondences. For a scalar comparison they form K null scores, compute a critical threshold τ_α as the (1-α)-quantile of the combined set of observed+null scores, an add-one permutation p-value, and a 'calibrated score' s_cal = max((s_obs - τ_α)/(s_max - τ_α), 0). For layer-wise comparisons across model layers, they apply aggregation-aware calibration: the same permutation is applied across all layers consistently, and an aggregate statistic T (e.g., max) is computed under the null to calibrate the selection step.
sections.theory: Proposition 4.1 shows that under independence, the expected Frobenius norm squared of the centered cross-covariance is d_x d_y/(n-1), giving CKA a null baseline of O(d/n). Proposition 4.2 shows that under independence, expected mKNN equals k/(n-1), so neighborhood metrics have null baseline O(k/n). For depth, a sub-Gaussian union bound shows E[T_max] ≤ μ + Cσ√log M with M=L_A L_B, demonstrating the multiple-comparisons inflation. Proofs of validity (super-uniformity of permutation p-values) and monotone invariance are provided.
sections.experiments: Synthetic experiments under H0 and H1 with Gaussian/heavy-tailed noise sweep n and d to validate that calibration removes width-driven null inflation. Type-I error control and power curves are shown. Synthetic two-model layer-stack experiments validate that aggregation-aware calibration removes depth confounding. The authors then revisit PRH using 1024 WIT image-text pairs, with three language model families (Bloomz, OpenLLaMA, LLaMA) and five vision model families (ImageNet-21K, MAE, DINOv2, CLIP, CLIP-finetuned), across 204 model pairs, evaluating CKA (linear/RBF), mKNN, cycle-kNN, CKNNA. They also extend to video-language alignment with VideoMAE.
sections.results: Synthetic: Raw CKA scores grow with d/n while calibrated scores stay at zero; Type-I error remains at or below α=0.05; power increases with signal strength. Calibrated CKA closely matches analytical debiased CKA (Murphy et al.). PRH: After calibration, global CKA shows no scaling trend with model capacity, while local mKNN/cycle-kNN/CKNNA retain a clear scaling trend across model families. The same pattern holds for video-language alignment. Locality analysis shows mKNN alignment exists at all k values (most pronounced at small k), while CKA-RBF at very small bandwidth (σ=0.1) shows no calibrated alignment - suggesting models agree on neighbor identity (topological) but not on exact local distances (metric).
sections.limitations: Permutation calibration assumes exchangeability (Assumption 3.1); grouped/clustered samples may violate it, requiring restricted permutations. The Aristotelian conclusion is drawn under the specific settings considered (n=1024, particular model families).
sections.conclusion: Representational similarity metrics suffer from width-dependent null baselines and depth-dependent selection inflation. A unified permutation-based null-calibration framework corrects both. Applying it to PRH shows global spectral convergence is largely confounded, while local neighborhood alignment is robust, motivating the Aristotelian Representation Hypothesis: networks converge to shared local neighborhood relationships.

main_claims: Spectral representational similarity metrics like CKA have non-zero null baselines that scale as O(d/n), confounding comparisons across models of different widths.

Selection-based summaries (e.g., max over layer pairs) inflate with the number of layer-pair comparisons due to multiple-comparisons effects, confounding comparisons across models of different depths.
A permutation-based null-calibration framework can be applied to any similarity metric to remove both confounders with finite-sample statistical guarantees (super-uniform p-values).
After calibration, the previously reported global cross-modal convergence under spectral metrics (CKA) largely disappears.
After calibration, local neighborhood-based metrics (mKNN, cycle-kNN, CKNNA) retain significant cross-modal alignment that scales with model capacity.
Cross-modal representations converge in topological neighborhood structure (who is near whom) but not in exact local metric distances, supporting the Aristotelian Representation Hypothesis.
key_contributions: Identification and formal characterization of two confounders (width via d/n null baseline; depth via look-elsewhere effect) in representational similarity comparison.
A metric-agnostic permutation-based null-calibration framework producing calibrated scores with finite-sample Type-I control.
An aggregation-aware extension that calibrates selection-based summaries (e.g., max over layer pairs) via consistent layer-wise permutations.
Theoretical results: Proposition 4.1 (CKA null baseline O(d/n)), Proposition 4.2 (mKNN null baseline k/(n-1)), and sub-Gaussian maximal inequality for the depth confounder.
Empirical re-evaluation of the Platonic Representation Hypothesis showing global convergence is largely confounded while local neighborhood convergence is robust.
Proposed Aristotelian Representation Hypothesis: representations converge to shared local neighborhood relationships.
experimental_results: Synthetic H0 sweeps: raw CKA grows with d/n; calibrated scores collapse to zero across Gaussian, Student-t, Laplace, and mixture distributions.
Type-I error remains at or below α=0.05 across (n, d/n) configurations; power increases rapidly with signal strength.
Calibrated CKA closely matches the analytical debiased CKA estimator of Murphy et al. without metric-specific derivation.
Synthetic depth experiment: raw max-CKA grows with number of layers L (1→128) under H0; aggregation-aware calibration removes this; entry-wise calibration alone does not.

PRH revisit on 204 vision-language model pairs: calibrated CKA (linear/RBF) shows no scaling trend with language model capacity, while calibrated mKNN/cycle-kNN/CKNNA retain clear scaling trends across all five vision families.
Video-language alignment (VideoMAE vs Bloom/OpenLLaMA/LLaMA): calibrated CKA shows no trend; calibrated mKNN shows clear trend especially with VideoMAE-Huge.

Locality analysis: mKNN alignment significant at all k∈{10,20,50,100} (strongest at small k); CKA-RBF with σ=0.1 (very local metric) shows no significant calibrated alignment, indicating topological rather than metric convergence.
Conclusions robust to α∈{0.01, 0.05, 0.10} and to permutation budgets K≥200.
assumptions_and_limitations: Exchangeability assumption (Assumption 3.1): n row pairs are exchangeable under H0; violated for grouped/clustered samples without restricted permutations.
Theoretical width derivations assume i.i.d. rows with mean 0 and identity covariance.
Maximal inequality assumes uniform sub-Gaussian right tails (Assumption D.5); empirical inflation in dependent settings is bounded but may be loose.
PRH experiments use n=1024 WIT samples and specific vision/language families; conclusions are stated as holding 'under the considered setting'.
Calibrated score normalization is not monotone-invariant by design, which is necessary to correct scale-dependent baselines but means different scaling choices give different effect sizes.
figures_tables: Figure 1: Conceptual illustration of the Aristotelian Representation Hypothesis - shared local neighborhoods across two spaces.
Figure 2: Summary of width and depth confounders and how calibration removes them; shows CKA loses convergence trend while mKNN retains it after calibration.
Figure 3: Heatmaps of raw vs calibrated scores across (n, d) under H0 for CKA, mKNN, CCA - calibrated scores collapse to zero.
Figure 4: Type-I error stays at/below α=0.05 across d/n; power curves rise with signal strength.
Figure 5: Aggregation-aware calibration: raw max-CKA inflates with layer count under H0; calibrated aggregates are stable; entry-wise calibration insufficient.
Figure 6: Revisiting PRH - calibrated CKA RBF (a) shows no scaling trend with LM capacity while raw scores do; calibrated mKNN (b) retains scaling trend.
Figure 7: Video-language alignment - same pattern as image-language: CKA flat after calibration, mKNN retains trend with VideoMAE-Huge.

Table 1 (App. B): Comparison with prior calibration approaches (Murphy, Chun, Cui, Diedrichsen, Cai, Smilde) showing only the proposed method covers any bounded metric, bounded output, and aggregation-awareness.

Figures 8-25 (appendix): Phase diagrams for different noise types, SNR sweeps, permutation budget analysis, full PRH results across all vision families and metrics, locality analyses for k and σ, sensitivity to α.
equations_algorithms: Eq. (2): Centered cross-covariance operator C = X_c^T Y_c/(n-1) used to define spectral metrics.
Eq. (3) / Prop. 4.1: E_H0[||C||_F^2] = d_x d_y/(n-1), establishing O(d/n) null baseline for CKA.
Eq. (4) / Prop. 4.2: E_H0[mKNN(X,Y)] = k/(n-1), establishing O(k/n) null baseline for neighborhood metrics.
Eq. (5)-(7): Sub-Gaussian tail bound and maximal inequality E[T_max] ≤ μ + Cσ√log M characterizing depth confounder.
Eq. (8)-(10): Construction of permutation null scores, rank-based critical value τ_α, and add-one permutation p-value.
Eq. (12): Calibrated score s_cal = max((s_obs - τ_α)/(s_max - τ_α), 0).
Eq. (14)-(17): Aggregation-aware calibration with consistent permutations across layers, p_agg, and T_cal.
Algorithm 1: Scalar Null Calibration - permutation procedure for a single similarity comparison.
Algorithm 2: Aggregation-Aware Null Calibration - extends to layer-wise comparisons with consistent permutations across all layers.
Corollary 5.1 / Lemma D.2: Super-uniformity of permutation p-values guaranteeing finite-sample Type-I control.

parsed.field_and_subfield: Machine learning; representation learning and representational similarity analysis (with connections to multimodal learning and statistical inference)
parsed.problem_addressed: Existing representational similarity metrics (CKA, CCA, RSA, mKNN, etc.) used to compare neural network representations across architectures and modalities are confounded by network scale: model width inflates spectral metrics via non-zero null baselines that scale with d/n, and model depth inflates max-aggregated similarity via multiple-comparisons effects. These confounders undermine claims of representational convergence such as the Platonic Representation Hypothesis (PRH).
parsed.research_question: Once representational similarity scores are properly calibrated against a principled null of no relationship, does the cross-modal convergence reported under the Platonic Representation Hypothesis still hold, and if so, in what form?
parsed.one_paragraph_summary: The paper argues that widely-used representational similarity metrics are systematically biased by network width (via O(d/n) null baselines for spectral metrics like CKA) and network depth (via look-elsewhere inflation when reporting max over layer pairs). The authors introduce a metric-agnostic permutation-based null-calibration framework that constructs an empirical null by shuffling sample correspondences, defines a critical threshold τ_α as a (1-α)-quantile of the combined observed+null scores, and rescales raw scores into a bounded calibrated effect size with finite-sample super-uniform p-values. An aggregation-aware variant applies the same permutation consistently across all layers and calibrates the entire reported summary statistic (e.g., max). Theoretically, they prove the d/n null baseline for CKA, the k/(n-1) null baseline for mKNN, and a sub-Gaussian maximal inequality for the depth effect. Empirically, synthetic experiments confirm width and depth bias removal with controlled Type-I error and preserved power, and the calibrated CKA closely matches Murphy et al.'s analytical debiased CKA. Applying the framework to PRH (204 vision-language pairs across BLOOM/OpenLLaMA/LLaMA and five vision families, plus video-language with VideoMAE) shows that the previously reported global CKA convergence largely vanishes, while local neighborhood metrics (mKNN, cycle-kNN, CKNNA) retain a robust scaling trend. Further locality analysis indicates topological rather than metric agreement (small-σ CKA-RBF shows no calibrated alignment), motivating the proposed 'Aristotelian Representation Hypothesis': networks converge to shared local neighborhood relationships.
parsed.problem_statement: Quantifying representational similarity across neural networks is central to understanding learned representations and to claims like the Platonic Representation Hypothesis, but raw similarity scores have non-trivial null expectations that depend on representation dimensionality (width) and on the size of the layer-pair search space (depth). This makes cross-scale comparisons unreliable and may produce spurious evidence of convergence. The paper aims to formalize these confounders and provide a principled, metric-agnostic correction.
main_contributions: Formal identification and characterization of two confounders in representational similarity: a width-driven null baseline (Proposition 4.1: E[||C̃||_F^2] = d_x d_y/(n-1) for CKA-type metrics) and a depth-driven multiple-comparisons inflation (sub-Gaussian maximal inequality E[T_max] ≤ μ + Cσ√log M).

A unified permutation-based null-calibration framework applicable to any similarity metric, producing a calibrated bounded effect size and an add-one permutation p-value with finite-sample super-uniformity (Type-I control, Corollary 5.1).

An aggregation-aware extension that calibrates selection summaries (max/top-k over layer pairs) by applying consistent permutations across layers, addressing the depth confounder directly at the level of the reported statistic.
Proposition 4.2 showing that neighborhood metrics (mKNN) have a much milder null baseline of k/(n-1), explaining why local metrics are less confounded.

Empirical re-evaluation of the Platonic Representation Hypothesis on 204 vision-language model pairs (plus video-language with VideoMAE), showing global spectral convergence largely disappears after calibration while local neighborhood convergence persists across model families, modalities, choices of α, and permutation budgets.

A proposed refinement, the Aristotelian Representation Hypothesis: representations converge to shared local neighborhood relationships ('who is near whom'), reflecting topological rather than metric alignment (supported by absence of calibrated alignment for small-bandwidth CKA-RBF).

claim_evidence_map.claim: Spectral similarity metrics like CKA have non-zero null baselines that scale as O(d/n).
claim_evidence_map.evidence_in_paper: Proposition 4.1 derives E_H0[||C̃||_F^2] = d_x d_y/(n-1) (proof in Section D.4); Figure 3 shows raw CKA increasing with d/n on H0 synthetic data; calibrated CKA matches Murphy et al.'s analytical debiased CKA (Figure 12, Section F.4).

claim_evidence_map.claim: Selection-based summaries (max over layer pairs) inflate with the number of layer pairs, confounding depth comparisons.
claim_evidence_map.evidence_in_paper: Section 4.2 derives a sub-Gaussian maximal inequality E[T_max] ≤ μ + Cσ√log M; Figure 5 shows raw max-CKA grows from L=1 to L=128 under H0 while aggregation-aware calibration removes this and naive entry-wise calibration does not.

claim_evidence_map.claim: The permutation-based calibration provides finite-sample Type-I control while preserving sensitivity to genuine signal.
claim_evidence_map.evidence_in_paper: Lemma D.2 and Corollary 5.1 establish super-uniformity; Figure 4 shows empirical Type-I error at or below α=0.05 across (n, d/n) and power increasing with signal strength; SNR sweeps in Section F.2 corroborate this.

claim_evidence_map.claim: Neighborhood metrics have a much smaller null baseline (k/(n-1)).
claim_evidence_map.evidence_in_paper: Proposition 4.2 / Theorem D.9 derives E_H0[mKNN] = k/(n-1) via hypergeometric argument; Figure 3 shows raw mKNN does not drift strongly with d.

claim_evidence_map.claim: After calibration, the global cross-modal convergence trend reported under CKA largely disappears.
claim_evidence_map.evidence_in_paper: Figure 6a and Section 6.3: dotted (raw) lines show increasing CKA with LM capacity while solid (calibrated) lines are flat; extended results across all five vision families and CKA variants confirm this (Figures 16-17, Section F.7); the same holds for video-language (Figure 7).

claim_evidence_map.claim: Local neighborhood metrics retain significant cross-modal alignment that scales with model capacity after calibration.
claim_evidence_map.evidence_in_paper: Figure 6b shows calibrated mKNN retains its upward trend with LM capacity across BLOOM, OpenLLaMA, LLaMA against DINOv2 and CLIP; Section F.7 (Figures 16, 17) extends this to cycle-kNN, CKNNA across five vision families; Figure 7 confirms it on video-language.

claim_evidence_map.claim: Cross-modal convergence is topological (neighbor identity) rather than metric (exact local distances).
claim_evidence_map.evidence_in_paper: Section F.9: mKNN shows alignment at k=10,20,50,100 (Figure 20), but CKA-RBF with σ=0.1 (very local kernel) shows calibrated scores near zero and non-significant p-values (Figures 21, 22), indicating no agreement on exact pairwise distances; larger σ gives significant but non-scaling alignment.

claim_evidence_map.claim: Conclusions are robust to design choices (α, K, noise distributions).
claim_evidence_map.evidence_in_paper: Section F.10 (Figures 23-25) shows invariance across α∈{0.01,0.05,0.10}; Section F.5 (Figure 13) shows stability for K≥200; Section F.1 (Figure 8) shows the calibration works across Gaussian/Student-t/Laplace/Gaussian-mixture noise.

sections.name: Abstract
sections.summary: States that representational similarity metrics are confounded by network width and depth, introduces a permutation-based null-calibration framework that produces calibrated scores with statistical guarantees, and reports that after calibration global spectral convergence disappears while local neighborhood agreement persists, motivating the 'Aristotelian Representation Hypothesis'.
key_claims: Width and depth systematically inflate raw representational similarity scores.
A permutation-based calibration framework yields calibrated scores with statistical guarantees.
After calibration, global convergence largely disappears but local neighborhood similarity remains significant cross-modally.
Representations converge to shared local neighborhood relationships, not global structure.
supporting_evidence: Forward references to Figure 2 and empirical results in Section 6.
issues: The phrase 'with statistical guarantees' is unspecified in the abstract; the nature of the guarantee (finite-sample Type-I control of p-values) is only clarified later.
'Apparent convergence largely disappears' is qualitative; no quantitative threshold or effect-size criterion is stated.
missing_information: No mention of the sample size (n=1024) or model regime under which the conclusion is drawn, which is later acknowledged as relevant to interpretation.
ambiguous_or_unsupported: 'Local neighborhood similarity, but not local distances' is a strong distinction introduced without preview of supporting evidence.

sections.name: Introduction
sections.summary: Motivates representational similarity, frames the PRH, and identifies two confounders: width (positive null baseline scaling with d/n for spectral metrics) and depth (look-elsewhere inflation when summarizing layer-pair comparisons via max). Previews calibration and the Aristotelian Representation Hypothesis.
key_claims: Spectral interaction-matrix-based metrics have positive null baselines that depend on d and n.
Selection-based summaries over many layer pairs inflate with the number of comparisons.
These confounders undermine comparative use of representational similarity without calibration.
supporting_evidence: Figure 2 conceptual schematic; subsequent theoretical sections (4) and empirical sections (6) cited.
issues: The claim that 'wider models can appear more aligned simply because their representations live in higher-dimensional spaces' implicitly assumes consistent preprocessing; effects depend on normalization (kernel CKA's bias depends nontrivially on centering and bandwidth), which is glossed over here.

The 'depth confounder' framing assumes practitioners use max-aggregation; the introduction does not acknowledge that Huh et al. and others sometimes use alternative summaries (best-matching layer-pair, top-k), which the framework handles but should be flagged upfront.
missing_information: No mention that the Platonic paper of Huh et al. itself used mKNN for the main result (not CKA), which is relevant to whether the calibrated reanalysis actually overturns Huh's main claim.
ambiguous_or_unsupported: The named distinction 'Platonic vs Aristotelian' is more of a rhetorical framing than a technical contribution; its philosophical motivation (footnote on Aristotle's Categories) is decorative.

sections.name: Related Work
sections.summary: Reviews representational similarity metrics, prior debiasing work (Murphy et al., Chun et al.), deconfounding (Cui et al.), and resampling-based maxT procedures. Positions the proposed framework as the first metric-agnostic, aggregation-aware calibration.
key_claims: Prior debiasing methods are metric-specific (e.g., CKA only).
No analogous debiasing exists for neighborhood metrics.
Resampling-based maxT procedures exist in statistics but have not been applied to representational similarity.
supporting_evidence: Citations to Murphy et al. 2024, Chun et al. 2025, Cui et al. 2022, Diedrichsen et al. 2020, Westfall & Young 1993, Nichols & Holmes 2002.
issues: Permutation testing for representational similarity is not novel in itself; Ding et al. (2021), cited but not engaged with deeply, used statistical testing for representation similarity. The paper should more clearly articulate what is technically new relative to existing permutation-based testing in the area.
Cai et al. (2019) bias-correction for RSA is glossed as 'partial' in Table 1 without explanation.
missing_information: No discussion of HSIC unbiased estimator (Song et al. 2012) as a form of debiasing, although it is referenced later in the appendix.
No comparison to recent work on debiased neighborhood metrics or to the distance-correlation literature, which faces similar high-dimensional bias issues.
ambiguous_or_unsupported: The claim that the framework is the only one with 'Bounded' output (Table 1) is a property of choice of rescaling, not a fundamental capability; comparable methods could be rescaled similarly.

sections.name: Method
sections.summary: Defines representation spaces and similarity scores, the permutation null H0 with exchangeability assumption, and the baseline problem of non-zero null expectations. Introduces scalar null-calibrated similarity and aggregation-aware null-calibration with consistent permutations across layers.
key_claims: Permutation under H0 provides a principled null distribution.
Scalar calibration via gating against τ_α produces a meaningful zero point.
Aggregation-aware calibration calibrates the entire selection pipeline by applying the same permutation across all layers.
supporting_evidence: Corollary 5.1 (Type-I control); Lemma D.2; Algorithm 1 and 2 in appendix.
issues: The calibrated score (Eq. 12) is not monotone-invariant and depends on α; the paper acknowledges this but it can be confusing because the rank-based p-value is monotone-invariant while the effect-size score is not, and reported 'calibrated scores' in figures are α-dependent.
The choice of s_max is implicit (e.g., 1 for CKA/mKNN). For unbounded metrics this is more arbitrary; the section briefly addresses this with [s − τ_α]_+ but the asymmetry is not fully discussed.

Consistency of permutations across layers (Eq. 14) assumes that within-model layer pairs are not themselves shuffled. This is correct, but the paper does not discuss whether this preserves the dependence structure between layers within a model (it does, but worth stating).
missing_information: The method does not discuss how to choose α in practice or how the calibrated effect size should be interpreted across metrics with different scales of baseline noise.
ambiguous_or_unsupported: The claim 'the calibrated score adapts to the actual null distribution, providing a meaningful zero point' is correct but glosses over the fact that s_cal still depends on the scale of (s_max − τ_α), which can differ substantially across metrics, making cross-metric comparison of calibrated scores nontrivial.

sections.name: Theory
sections.summary: Provides Proposition 4.1 (CKA null baseline O(d/n)), Proposition 4.2 (mKNN null baseline k/(n-1)), and a sub-Gaussian maximal inequality for depth-driven inflation. Supplementary appendix proves super-uniformity of permutation p-values and monotone invariance.
key_claims: Under H0, E[||C̃||_F^2] = d_x d_y/(n-1), so CKA has a positive null baseline scaling as O(d/n).
Under H0, E[mKNN] = k/(n-1), so neighborhood metrics have null baseline O(k/n).
Under sub-Gaussian right tails, E[T_max] ≤ μ + Cσ√log M, formalizing depth inflation.
supporting_evidence: Detailed proofs in Section D (D.4 for Prop 4.1, D.5 for depth, D.6 for Prop 4.2).
issues: Prop 4.1 establishes a baseline for ||C̃||_F^2, but linear CKA is normalized by ||Σ_XX||_F · ||Σ_YY||_F. The text says 'CKA is scaled by the normalized self-similarity terms, which each scale as O(√d), resulting in null baseline O(d/n)' — this informal argument elides the random-matrix fluctuations of the denominator and assumes specific scalings; a more careful statement would help.

Prop 4.2 derivation requires k-NN sets to be uniformly distributed over k-subsets, which holds under i.i.d. assumptions but may not hold under realistic representation distributions (e.g., low intrinsic dimension structure). This is briefly noted but not quantified.

The sub-Gaussian assumption (D.5) on layer-pair similarities is unverified; bounded scores get a sub-Gaussian bound via Hoeffding but with σ ≤ (s_max − s_min)/2, which is loose. Dependence across layers (which is strong in practice) means union bound is conservative — the paper acknowledges this but does not characterize the effective number of comparisons.
missing_information: No analysis of what happens for unbiased CKA, which corrects the d/n bias analytically — does calibration still meaningfully change it? The appendix touches on this but the main theory section does not.
No characterization of the variance of mKNN under H0 beyond appendix Prop D.10, which gives bounds rather than the realistic case where per-anchor dependencies exist.
ambiguous_or_unsupported: The remark that 'closed-form bulk edges are ensemble- and normalization-specific' justifies the permutation approach but is not formally argued; alternative analytical corrections (e.g., Murphy et al.) are shown to match the empirical calibration in Section F.4, somewhat undermining the strength of the argument that closed-form is brittle.

sections.name: Experiments
sections.summary: Validates calibration on synthetic data under H0 (Gaussian and heavy-tailed) and H1 (low-rank signal), shows Type-I error control and power, validates aggregation-aware calibration on synthetic layer-stack experiments, and revisits PRH with 204 vision-language model pairs and video-language alignment with VideoMAE.
key_claims: Calibration eliminates spurious similarity across metrics, sample sizes, dimensions, and noise distributions.
Type-I error is controlled at or below α=0.05; power increases rapidly with signal strength.
Aggregation-aware calibration removes depth-driven inflation; entry-wise calibration is insufficient.
On PRH, calibrated global metrics show no scaling trend with model capacity while calibrated local metrics retain scaling trends across vision families.
Video-language alignment shows the same local vs global pattern.
supporting_evidence: Figures 3-7; extensive ablations in appendix Sections F.1-F.10.
issues: The synthetic experiments use Gaussian/heavy-tailed i.i.d. data, which does not capture the structured, low-intrinsic-dimension nature of real representations. The validation that calibration 'works' on i.i.d. synthetic data may be over-stating generalization.

The number of WIT samples (n=1024) is small relative to embedding dimensions in modern models. Combined with d/n ∈ [0.75, 8], the calibration framework is being applied in a regime where null baselines are substantial — but it would be more compelling to also test with larger n to show that conclusions are not sample-size artifacts.

The synthetic depth experiment uses linear CKA with d/n=8 and independent layer representations across both models. Real models have strong layer-to-layer correlations, so the demonstrated 'inflation' is the worst case; how much of the inflation observed in actual PRH experiments is attributable to depth vs width is not quantified separately.

Power analysis (Fig 4 right) is shown for synthetic signals but not for realistic representation structure; it is unclear how the framework discriminates weak-but-real signals from null at finite n in real model comparisons.
The video-language experiment uses only 3 VideoMAE sizes against 3 LM families, which is a small base for the scaling claim.
missing_information: Calibrated CKA results are not directly compared to unbiased CKA (Song et al.) or debiased CKA (Murphy et al.) on the actual PRH data; only matched on synthetic data in F.4. A direct comparison on real models would strengthen the claim that the disappearance of CKA scaling is not specific to the permutation approach.

No analysis of whether the local vs global distinction holds for vision-vision or language-language comparisons (within-modality), which would help establish whether this is a cross-modality phenomenon or a general property.
No baseline comparison showing that random/untrained models give no calibrated alignment — this would be a useful sanity check.
ambiguous_or_unsupported: The statement that 'smaller video encoders appear to act as a bottleneck, limiting alignment regardless of language model size' is interpretive and based on a single Figure 7 comparison; alternative explanations (e.g., domain mismatch) are not discussed.

sections.name: Results
sections.summary: The PRH revisit shows calibrated CKA loses its scaling trend while calibrated mKNN/cycle-kNN/CKNNA retain it. Locality analysis (F.9) shows mKNN alignment at all k but small-σ CKA-RBF shows no calibrated alignment, supporting topological (not metric) convergence.
key_claims: Calibrated global metrics show no systematic trend with model scale.
Calibrated local metrics retain significant scaling trends across all vision families.
Cross-modal models agree on neighborhood identity but not on exact local distances.
supporting_evidence: Figures 6-7 in main text; Figures 16-22 in appendix; p-value analysis in Figure 18.
issues: Figure 18 shows that all 204 model pairs are significant at p<0.05 for both global and local metrics, including CKA. This is a tension with the claim that 'global convergence largely disappears' — the calibrated effect size flattens but is non-zero and significant. The narrative could be more nuanced: it is the scaling trend that disappears, not the alignment itself.

The locality analysis (F.9) uses fixed σ values for the RBF kernel that depend on the (unstated) feature norms; without normalization or median-heuristic justification, claiming 'no significant alignment at σ=0.1' is ambiguous because the relevant scale depends on embedding magnitudes that differ across models.

The conclusion that local metric distances are not preserved while local neighborhood identity is preserved is a meaningful refinement, but it leans heavily on the σ=0.1 result; alternative tests (e.g., correlation of local distance ranks) would strengthen this distinction.
missing_information: Whether the calibrated mKNN scaling trend would survive if model pairs were re-ranked or if vision capability (not LM capability) was the x-axis variable.
Effect sizes of the scaling trend (e.g., slope of calibrated mKNN vs LM capacity) are not quantified.
How calibrated CKA compares against null even in absence of scaling — is the average calibrated CKA across model pairs zero, or just non-trending?
ambiguous_or_unsupported: The framing 'global convergence largely disappears' contrasts with 'all model pairs are significant at p<0.05 for global metrics' (Fig 18) — these statements need reconciliation in the main text.

sections.name: Limitations
sections.summary: Acknowledges that permutation calibration is finite-sample valid under exchangeability, which can be violated in grouped/clustered samples, and that restricted permutations can recover validity.
key_claims: Calibration validity depends on the exchangeability assumption.
issues: The limitations section is unusually brief and does not address several substantive concerns: (i) the dependence of conclusions on n=1024; (ii) the sensitivity of effect sizes to the chosen s_max and α; (iii) the assumption of i.i.d. structure in theoretical derivations not matching real representations; (iv) the fact that the proposed framework cannot distinguish between 'genuine convergence' and 'arbitrarily small but real convergence', since calibration only gates against null.
No discussion of computational cost for large-scale studies (K × L_A × L_B similarity computations).
missing_information: No discussion of failure modes of mKNN-based calibration when k is comparable to n (where the null baseline k/(n-1) is large).
No discussion of whether the locality findings extend to other modalities (audio, code, etc.).

sections.name: Conclusion
sections.summary: Summarizes the calibration framework and the Aristotelian Representation Hypothesis: representations converge to shared local neighborhood relationships, not global spectral structure.
key_claims: Width-dependent baselines and depth-dependent selection inflation distort representational similarity.
Calibrated effect sizes show global spectral convergence is largely confounded, while local neighborhood alignment is robust.
Models converge to shared local neighborhood relationships.
supporting_evidence: Recapitulation of empirical findings from Section 6 and Appendix F.
issues: The 'Aristotelian Representation Hypothesis' is presented as a refinement but is essentially a relabeling of the local-similarity finding that was already shown in Huh et al. (who used mKNN as their main metric). It is not clear how much is genuinely new claim vs. reframing.
The conclusion overstates by saying 'previously reported global spectral convergence is largely confounded' — Huh et al. did not primarily rely on global spectral metrics for their headline result.
missing_information: No discussion of implications for downstream tasks (transfer learning, brain-score) where representational similarity is used as a tool.
ambiguous_or_unsupported: The 'shared local neighborhood relationships' phrasing remains qualitative; no operational criterion (e.g., minimum k, threshold on calibrated mKNN) defines what counts as convergence.

sections.name: Supplementary
sections.summary: Provides detailed metric definitions, proofs (super-uniformity, monotone invariance, Prop 4.1 derivation, depth-confounder bound, mKNN null baseline), algorithm pseudocode, and extensive additional experiments including phase diagrams, SNR sweeps, calibration variant comparisons, comparison to Murphy/Chun debiasing, permutation budget analysis, full PRH results across all metrics and vision families, video-language analysis, locality (k and σ) analyses, and α-sensitivity.
key_claims: Calibration works across noise distributions and SNR regimes.
Empirical calibration recovers analytical debiased CKA (Murphy et al.) on synthetic data.
K≥200 permutations suffice for stable thresholds.
PRH conclusions hold across all five vision families and four similarity metric variants.
Locality analysis shows topological (rank-based) convergence but not metric (distance-based) convergence.
supporting_evidence: Figures 8-25; Theorem D.9 (mKNN null), Proposition D.10 (mKNN variance), Lemma D.2, Proposition D.4.
issues: Theorem D.9 (mKNN null baseline) implicitly requires that NX(i) and NY(i) are functions of independent random variables; the proof asserts this but the assumption that representations across modalities are mutually independent under H0 (which corresponds to a strong null) deserves more discussion.

Figure 18 shows that p-values for global CKA are uniformly small (<0.005), indicating significant cross-modal alignment exists for all model pairs. This is in some tension with the main-text framing that 'global convergence disappears'; the supplementary text acknowledges this and reframes as 'no convergence in calibrated effect sizes', which is a more accurate but weaker claim.

The bandwidth σ values for CKA-RBF (0.1, 0.5, 2.0, 5.0) are stated without justification of feature scales; the conclusion that σ=0.1 shows no significant alignment could be a result of σ being below the natural scale of the data rather than evidence about local metric structure.
F.5 recommends K≥200 but does not analyze the impact of small K on aggregation-aware calibration where the null distribution is more complex; the stability analysis is only for scalar comparisons.
missing_information: No analysis of computational scaling for very large models (e.g., 65B params); how does aggregation-aware calibration scale when L_A · L_B is large?
No comparison to alternative aggregation operators (e.g., mean over layer-pairs, top-k average) beyond Figure 5.
No analysis of whether different choices of distance for k-NN (Euclidean vs cosine) affect conclusions.
ambiguous_or_unsupported: F.9's strong claim that 'cross-modal representations agree on neighborhood identity but not on exact local distances' rests on a single set of σ values; alternative ways to test metric vs topological convergence (e.g., Procrustes on local neighborhoods, local distance correlations) are not used.

claims.claim: Linear/spectral CKA-type metrics have a non-zero null baseline that scales as O(d_x d_y / n) under independence.
claims.evidence_in_paper: Proposition 4.1 with proof in Section D.4 derives E_H0[||C̃||_F^2] = d_x d_y/(n-1) under i.i.d. zero-mean identity-covariance assumptions; Figure 3 shows raw CKA increasing with d/n.
claims.notes: Proof is straightforward and assumptions (i.i.d., identity covariance) are standard; consistent with known random matrix theory results.

claims.claim: Mutual k-NN has null baseline k/(n-1), much smaller than spectral baselines.
claims.evidence_in_paper: Proposition 4.2 / Theorem D.9 via hypergeometric argument (Proposition D.8); empirical confirmation in Figure 3.
claims.notes: Proof relies on exchangeability of i.i.d. samples; reasonable derivation.

claims.claim: Selection-based summaries (max over layer pairs) inflate as O(σ√log M) where M = L_A L_B.
claims.evidence_in_paper: Section 4.2 and Proposition D.6 derive sub-Gaussian maximal inequality without independence assumption; Figure 5 empirically demonstrates depth-driven inflation.
claims.notes: Classical result applied appropriately; the bound is dependence-robust which is important since layer similarities are correlated.

claims.claim: The permutation-based calibration provides finite-sample Type-I control (super-uniform p-values).
claims.evidence_in_paper: Lemma D.2 and Corollary 5.1 with proofs in Section D.1; empirical verification in Figure 4 (Type-I error ≤ α).
claims.notes: Standard randomization inference result; correctly applied. Requires exchangeability under H0 (Assumption 3.1).

claims.claim: Calibration preserves sensitivity to genuine signal (power).
claims.evidence_in_paper: Figure 4 right panel shows detection rates rapidly increase with signal strength across metrics; SNR sweeps (Figures 9, 10) show monotone decay with noise.
claims.notes: Synthetic only, but covers multiple metrics and signal ranks.

claims.claim: Empirical permutation calibration recovers analytical debiased CKA estimators.
claims.evidence_in_paper: Section F.4, Figure 12: difference between calibrated CKA and Murphy et al.'s debiased CKA is near zero across d/n.
claims.notes: Validates the framework against an independent metric-specific correction.

claims.claim: After calibration, global spectral convergence reported under PRH largely disappears.
claims.evidence_in_paper: Figure 6a, 7, and Section F.7 (Figures 16, 17) across five vision families and CKA variants show flat calibrated trends versus rising raw trends.
claims.notes: Comprehensive across families and metrics. However, 'largely disappears' is somewhat qualitative; some residual scaling may still be present visually.

claims.claim: Local neighborhood metrics retain significant cross-modal alignment that scales with model capacity even after calibration.
claims.evidence_in_paper: Figures 6b, 7, 16-18 show calibrated mKNN/cycle-kNN/CKNNA trends increasing with LM capacity; p-values significant for all 204 pairs.
claims.notes: Robust across families, metrics, modalities (image and video), and α choices.

claims.claim: Cross-modal convergence is topological (neighbor identity) rather than metric (exact distances).
claims.evidence_in_paper: Section F.9: small-σ CKA-RBF shows non-significant calibrated scores (Figures 21, 22), while mKNN at varying k shows significant alignment.
claims.notes: The argument hinges on contrasting CKA-RBF at small bandwidth with mKNN. At σ=0.1, raw scores are near 1.0 (saturation), which complicates interpretation—the lack of calibrated signal could reflect kernel saturation rather than absence of metric agreement. The interpretation that this proves 'topological vs. metric' is suggestive but not airtight.

claims.claim: The Aristotelian Representation Hypothesis: neural networks converge to shared local neighborhood relationships.
claims.evidence_in_paper: Proposed in introduction and Section 6.3 based on empirical findings; supported by mKNN scaling trends across image-language and video-language settings.
claims.notes: This is a refined hypothesis rather than a proven claim. The evidence supports persistence of local alignment after calibration, but 'convergence' as a strong claim depends on the rate and limit of the trend with scale. The empirical curves appear to be slowly increasing rather than clearly approaching a shared structure.

claims.claim: The framework is metric-agnostic and applies to any similarity metric.
claims.evidence_in_paper: Sections 5, E (Algorithms 1-2); empirical results across CKA (linear/RBF/unbiased), mKNN, cycle-kNN, CKNNA, RSA, CCA/SVCCA/PWCCA, RV, Procrustes.
claims.notes: Demonstrated across a broad range of metrics in Section F.6.

claims.claim: Calibration works across noise distributions without distributional assumptions.
claims.evidence_in_paper: Section F.1 (Figure 8) and Section F.6 (Figure 15) test Gaussian, Student-t, Laplace, and Gaussian mixture distributions.
claims.notes: Permutation-based methods are inherently distribution-free under exchangeability.

claims.claim: Conclusions are robust to choice of α and permutation budget K.
claims.evidence_in_paper: Section F.5 (Figure 13): stability by K≈200; Section F.10 (Figures 23-25): invariance to α ∈ {0.01, 0.05, 0.10}.

claims.claim: Existing approaches to debiasing representational similarity are metric-specific while ours is unified and metric-agnostic.
claims.evidence_in_paper: Related work and Table 1 in Appendix B compares prior corrections (Murphy 2024, Chun 2025, Cui 2022, Diedrichsen 2020, etc.).
claims.notes: Reasonable positioning; the permutation/maxT idea is classical in statistics but its systematic application to representational similarity, especially with aggregation-aware variants, appears novel.

claims.claim: Naive entry-wise calibration of layer-pair scores still leads to inflation when reporting a summary statistic.
claims.evidence_in_paper: Figure 5: entry-wise calibrated max still inflates with layer count, while aggregation-aware calibration is flat.
claims.notes: Clear and important demonstration motivating the aggregation-aware design.

claims.claim: The PRH analysis covers 204 vision-language model pairs spanning d/n ∈ [0.75, 8].
claims.evidence_in_paper: Section 6.3: WIT dataset with n=1024 samples, three LM families and five vision families.
claims.notes: Reasonably broad coverage following Huh et al. (2024)'s protocol.

unsupported_claims: The strong form of the 'Aristotelian Representation Hypothesis' (i.e., that representations are *converging* to shared local neighborhood relationships) is asserted but not rigorously established. The empirical results show persistent positive alignment with mild upward trends, but convergence in the strong sense (e.g., toward a common limit as scale increases) is not demonstrated.

The interpretation that 'convergence is topological rather than metric' relies primarily on CKA-RBF at σ=0.1 showing no calibrated alignment. However, at σ=0.1 the raw CKA approaches 1.0 (kernel saturation), so the calibrated zero may reflect a degenerate regime rather than absence of metric agreement.
overstated_claims: 'Apparent convergence reported by global spectral measures largely disappears after calibration': while the trend flattens, the calibrated scores remain non-zero and statistically significant; the claim could be more nuanced as 'the scaling trend disappears' rather than 'convergence disappears'.

Naming the refined hypothesis after Aristotle and framing it as a positive thesis may overstate what the data show: the local alignment is significant but its magnitudes (often 0.10-0.20 for calibrated mKNN) are modest, and the upward scaling is gradual.
claims_requiring_external_verification: Whether the d/n null baseline derivation for CKA is consistent with prior work by Murphy et al. (2024) and Chun et al. (2025) under matching assumptions.

Whether the reproduction of Huh et al. (2024)'s PRH results uses the same sample size (n=1024) and embedding extraction protocol as the original work, and whether the reported raw trends quantitatively match.
Whether the Type-I error guarantee under Assumption 3.1 (exchangeability) holds in practice given WIT data may have group/topic clustering structure violating strict exchangeability.

novelty_axes.claimed_contribution: A metric-agnostic permutation-based null-calibration framework that transforms any representational similarity metric into a calibrated score with finite-sample Type-I guarantees, including an aggregation-aware variant that calibrates selection statistics (e.g., max over layer pairs).
novelty_axes.why_it_matters: The framework is positioned as the key technical contribution that unifies bias correction across metrics and extends to selection-based summaries; if classical permutation/maxT had already been applied to representational similarity, the methodological novelty would be modest.
