similar_prior_work.title: Grounding Representation Similarity Through Statistical Testing
similar_prior_work.similarity_summary: Ding, Denain, Steinhardt propose a statistical testing framework to evaluate representational similarity measures, scoring measures by their sensitivity and specificity to functional changes in networks.
similar_prior_work.key_difference: Ding et al. use statistical testing to *benchmark and select* similarity metrics; the present paper uses permutation testing to *calibrate* any given metric's output (subtracting a null baseline) and explicitly addresses width and depth confounders in cross-scale comparisons. Different goal — benchmarking metrics vs. correcting reported scores.

similar_prior_work.title: Resampling-based Multiple Testing: Examples and Methods for p-value Adjustment / Nonparametric Permutation Tests for Functional Neuroimaging
similar_prior_work.similarity_summary: Westfall & Young's maxT and Nichols & Holmes's permutation procedures are the classical templates for calibrating max-statistics under multiple comparisons via permutation.
similar_prior_work.key_difference: Classical methods. The paper itself explicitly notes these have not previously been applied to representational similarity selection (max over layer pairs); the contribution is the application/adaptation, not the statistical technique itself.

similar_prior_work.title: Representational Similarity Analysis – Connecting the Branches of Systems Neuroscience
similar_prior_work.similarity_summary: Kriegeskorte et al. introduce RSA and routinely use permutation testing of the stimulus labels to assess significance of RDM correlations.
similar_prior_work.key_difference: Permutation-based significance for RSA is standard; this paper generalizes the idea to any bounded similarity (including CKA, mKNN, CKNNA, CCA-family) and adds an aggregation-aware extension for layer-pair selection, plus a max-preserving effect-size rescaling.

similar_prior_work.title: Correcting Biased Centered Kernel Alignment Measures in Biological and Artificial Neural Networks
similar_prior_work.similarity_summary: Murphy, Zylberberg, Fyshe derive an analytical debiased CKA that removes the d-dependent inflation, demonstrating that biased CKA can be artificially high when feature dimensionalities differ.
similar_prior_work.key_difference: Murphy et al. give a metric-specific analytic correction for CKA only; the present work delivers a permutation-based correction that the authors show empirically recovers Murphy's debiased CKA (Section F.4) while applying generically to mKNN, CKA, CKNNA, RSA, etc., and additionally handling the depth/selection confounder.

similar_prior_work.title: Estimating Neural Representation Alignment from Sparsely Sampled Inputs and Features
similar_prior_work.similarity_summary: Chun, Canatar, Chung, Lee propose corrections for CKA that account for finite-sample and feature-sampling effects (including column-dependence).
similar_prior_work.key_difference: Like Murphy et al., this is metric-specific. The paper compares to dep-cols CKA (Section F.4) and shows the permutation calibration is closer to Murphy's debiased CKA on independent-column synthetic data; the present framework is metric-agnostic and adds aggregation-aware calibration.

similar_prior_work.title: Position: The Platonic Representation Hypothesis
similar_prior_work.similarity_summary: Huh, Cheung, Wang, Isola argue that representations across modalities and scales are converging to a shared statistical model of reality, supported by both global (CKA) and local (mKNN) similarity trends with model capacity.
similar_prior_work.key_difference: This paper is the direct target of the critique. The present work argues the global-CKA evidence is largely a width/depth confounding artifact and refines the hypothesis to local neighborhood relationships only ('Aristotelian').

similar_prior_work.title: Do Vision and Language Encoders Represent the World Similarly?
similar_prior_work.similarity_summary: Maniparambil et al. study cross-modal alignment of vision and language encoders, reporting evidence of representational similarity.
similar_prior_work.key_difference: Reports raw similarity trends; does not control for the d/n null baseline or selection inflation. The present work re-evaluates such trends with calibration.

similar_prior_work.title: Deconfounded Representation Similarity for Comparison of Neural Networks
similar_prior_work.similarity_summary: Cui, Kumar, Marttinen, Kaski propose regression-style deconfounding to remove the effect of shared input-population structure from RSA/CKA scores.
similar_prior_work.key_difference: Addresses a different confounder (shared input statistics across networks) rather than the dimensionality/scale-driven null baselines and selection inflation that this paper targets; not metric-agnostic nor aggregation-aware.

similar_prior_work.title: Matrix Correlations for High-Dimensional Data: the Modified RV-Coefficient
similar_prior_work.similarity_summary: Smilde et al. introduce the modified/adjusted RV coefficient with a chance correction for high-dimensional regimes.
similar_prior_work.key_difference: Provides a metric-specific (RV) chance correction by subtracting an estimated expectation; the present paper provides a general permutation-based correction (bounded, max-preserving, aggregation-aware) covering many metrics including RV.

potential_missing_citations: Westfall & Young 1993 and Nichols & Holmes 2002 are cited but mostly in passing; given the aggregation-aware calibration is essentially an application of maxT to layer-pair selection, a more thorough technical comparison (rather than a one-sentence note that 'this has not been applied in representational similarity studies') would be appropriate.

Klabunde et al. (Similarity of Neural Network Models: A Survey) is cited, but other systematic critiques of CKA's reliability under dimensionality changes (e.g., Davari et al. 'Reliability of CKA as a Similarity Measure', 2022/2023) could be mentioned when motivating the width confounder.

Work on the look-elsewhere effect / family-wise error control in deep-learning representational analyses appears to be cited only via Westfall & Young and Bonferroni; specific recent work applying these in neuroscience-AI comparisons (e.g., brain-score-style analyses) could strengthen the related work.
parsed.rationale: The paper's technical machinery is a careful but largely classical assembly: permutation testing for similarity statistics is standard in RSA (Kriegeskorte 2008+), and the aggregation-aware calibration of a max-statistic is exactly the maxT idea of Westfall & Young (1993) / Nichols & Holmes (2002). The paper acknowledges this and notes the gap is in *application* to representational similarity at scale. The theoretical results are also derivative: the O(d/n) null baseline of CKA aligns with known random-matrix-theory phenomena and with concurrent analytic debiased-CKA derivations by Murphy et al. (2024) and Chun et al. (2025), and the mKNN null = k/(n-1) is a simple hypergeometric calculation. The sub-Gaussian max inequality is textbook. Thus the method and theory axes are incremental rather than foundational. Where the paper is genuinely novel is empirical and conceptual: (i) it is, to my knowledge, the first systematic, scale-controlled re-examination of the Platonic Representation Hypothesis showing that global CKA trends are largely confounded while local neighborhood trends are not; (ii) the topological-vs-metric locality dissection (varying mKNN-k and CKA-RBF bandwidth) is a fresh empirical observation, even though one of its interpretations (small-σ CKA = 0) may be partly driven by kernel saturation; and (iii) the 'Aristotelian Representation Hypothesis' is a new conceptual framing of a known phenomenon. The overall package therefore earns a 'moderate' novelty rating — incremental on technique and theory, but a meaningful and timely empirical contribution that materially refines an actively discussed hypothesis in the field. Risk is medium because reviewers familiar with classical multiple-testing or with the debiased-CKA literature may judge the methodological contribution as repackaging known statistics.

domain_expert.add_to_field: The paper makes a substantive conceptual contribution by reframing the Platonic Representation Hypothesis: it provides evidence that purported cross-modal convergence under global spectral metrics is largely an artifact of width/depth scaling, while local neighborhood (topological) agreement survives calibration. This nuanced 'Aristotelian' refinement reshapes how the community should interpret convergence claims and reconnects representational similarity to its neuroscience roots (RSA-style topological comparisons).
domain_expert.beneficiaries: Researchers studying representation learning, multimodal alignment, model stitching/transfer, neuroscience-AI comparisons (Brain-Score, NeuroAI), and anyone making scaling claims based on CKA or similar metrics. Also benefits authors of follow-up PRH-style studies (video, brain, biological vision).
domain_expert.importance: Yes. The PRH has been highly influential, and many recent papers build on uncalibrated similarity comparisons. Showing that a core empirical pillar is partly confounded is an important corrective for the field.
domain_expert.improvement_meaningful: The qualitative shift from 'global convergence' to 'local topological convergence' is meaningful and reframes interpretation. The proposed Aristotelian framing is more defensible and aligns with prior intuitions from manifold learning.
domain_expert.future_influence: Likely to influence how future PRH-style and cross-model similarity studies are reported; could become a standard sanity check. Will also influence neuroscience-AI alignment literature, where CKA/RSA are dominant tools.

methodology_expert.add_to_field: A clean, metric-agnostic permutation-based calibration framework with finite-sample super-uniform p-values and an aggregation-aware extension. The unification of width-bias debiasing and depth/selection (look-elsewhere) correction in a single framework is novel in the representational similarity context, importing well-established randomization-inference tools (maxT, permutation tests) into a setting that lacked them.
methodology_expert.beneficiaries: Anyone reporting similarity scores across models/layers—not just PRH researchers. Practitioners doing model comparison, layer-pair search, brain-model alignment, or comparing self-supervised pretraining objectives.
methodology_expert.importance: Yes. The width baseline scaling as d/n and the maximum-over-layer-pairs inflation are real, documented issues, and existing fixes (debiased CKA) are metric-specific. A general, distribution-free, easy-to-implement framework fills a clear methodological gap.
methodology_expert.improvement_meaningful: Yes. The theoretical contributions (Prop 4.1, 4.2, sub-Gaussian maximal inequality) are mostly straightforward but correctly identify the scaling, and the permutation framework is principled. The agreement with Murphy et al.'s analytical debiased CKA is a strong validation.
methodology_expert.future_influence: High potential to become a standard tool/best practice. The code release and simple algorithms (Algs. 1-2) lower the adoption barrier. May influence reviewer expectations for similarity-based claims.

empirical_evaluation_expert.add_to_field: A thorough empirical re-evaluation of PRH across 204 vision-language pairs, multiple metric families, and an extension to video-language. The combination of controlled synthetic experiments (Type-I, power, phase diagrams) with large-scale real-model experiments and extensive ablations (α, K, noise distributions, neighborhood size, bandwidth) is exemplary.
empirical_evaluation_expert.beneficiaries: Empirical researchers benchmarking similarity-based claims; reviewers evaluating such claims.
empirical_evaluation_expert.importance: Yes—both validating the framework and re-running the PRH analysis are needed to credibly challenge a well-known hypothesis.
empirical_evaluation_expert.improvement_meaningful: The empirical evidence is convincing. The local-vs-global divergence is consistent across model families, modalities (image, video), metric variants (mKNN, cycle-kNN, CKNNA vs CKA linear/RBF/unbiased), and design choices, which strengthens the claim. The topological vs metric distinction (small-σ CKA-RBF analysis) is a particularly strong piece of evidence.
empirical_evaluation_expert.future_influence: Sets a higher bar for empirical PRH-style analyses; provides a replicable protocol others can adopt.

skeptical_reviewer.add_to_field: The methodological framework is largely a careful application of standard permutation/maxT inference to representational similarity, plus straightforward bias derivations. The novelty is in framing and application rather than statistical innovation. The 'Aristotelian' rebranding is partly rhetorical.
skeptical_reviewer.beneficiaries: Mostly PRH-adjacent researchers; broader ML may find the contribution incremental from a statistics standpoint.
skeptical_reviewer.importance: The width baseline is already known (Murphy et al. 2024 debiased CKA), and multiple-comparisons correction is textbook. The contribution is therefore more synthesis than discovery. However, the empirical re-examination of PRH is genuinely important.
skeptical_reviewer.improvement_meaningful: The calibrated CKA closely matches debiased CKA—suggesting the framework reproduces, rather than improves on, existing corrections for the spectral case. The main novel empirical claim (local convergence persists, global does not) is meaningful but rests on the assumption that the permutation null is the 'right' null; alternative debiasing schemes might give different verdicts. The choice of bounded rescaling to [0,1] is somewhat arbitrary. Sample size n=1024 is relatively small for d/n up to 8.
skeptical_reviewer.future_influence: Possibly moderate. If reviewers adopt calibration as a default sanity check, it could shift practice; otherwise, the Aristotelian framing may not displace PRH terminology.

practical_impact_expert.add_to_field: A drop-in, easy-to-implement calibration procedure with released code, applicable to any bounded similarity metric. This is practically valuable because researchers can apply it without metric-specific derivations.
practical_impact_expert.beneficiaries: Practitioners and researchers running model comparison studies in deep learning, multimodal AI, and computational neuroscience. Reviewers and authors who need a defensible null reference for similarity claims.
practical_impact_expert.importance: Yes—similarity metrics are pervasive in empirical ML and neuroscience, and the lack of principled null calibration is a real reproducibility/comparability concern.
practical_impact_expert.improvement_meaningful: Yes. Computational cost is modest (K=200-500 permutations × O(LA·LB) similarity computations), and the procedure parallelizes trivially. The aggregation-aware version is particularly useful for layer-search studies.
practical_impact_expert.future_influence: Likely to be adopted in PRH follow-ups, NeuroAI alignment work, and model auditing contexts. Could become a community-standard reporting practice within a few years.

consensus: The paper identifies and convincingly demonstrates real confounders (width-driven null baselines and depth-driven selection inflation) in representational similarity comparisons.
The permutation-based, metric-agnostic calibration framework is practical, easy to implement, and theoretically grounded with finite-sample guarantees.
The empirical re-evaluation of PRH is thorough and the local-vs-global distinction (topological vs metric alignment) is well-supported across model families, modalities, and design choices.
The proposed Aristotelian Representation Hypothesis is a more defensible refinement of PRH and is likely to influence future work.
The contribution is most novel as a synthesis—unifying width debiasing and depth/maxT calibration in a metric-agnostic way—rather than as new statistical theory.
disagreement: How novel the statistical methodology is: methodology and practical impact experts emphasize unification and applicability, while the skeptical reviewer views it as a straightforward application of standard permutation/maxT inference.
Whether the Aristotelian framing is a substantive reinterpretation (domain/empirical experts) or partly rhetorical rebranding (skeptic).
Whether the calibration adds value beyond existing debiased CKA: methodology experts see metric-agnostic generality as the win; skeptic notes the spectral case largely reproduces known corrections.

definitions_clear: Representation spaces, similarity score, and null hypothesis of independence are clearly defined (Section 3).
Scalar and aggregation-aware null-calibrated scores are formally defined with explicit formulas (Equations 9, 10, 12, 15-17).
Permutation procedure is operationally specified via Algorithms 1 and 2, with clear notation for permutations consistent across layers.
assumptions_clear: Exchangeability under the null (Assumption 3.1) is stated and used consistently in proofs.
Sub-Gaussian right tails for the depth maximal inequality (Assumption D.5) are explicitly stated.
i.i.d. assumptions for Proposition 4.1 and 4.2 are stated, though their realism for actual neural network activations is not discussed in the main text.
derivations_algorithms_clear: Algorithms 1 and 2 provide step-by-step pseudocode that is easy to implement.
Proofs of the null baselines (D.4, D.6) are explicit and follow standard arguments.
The depth-inflation derivation (D.5) is presented with clear steps and bound.
suggested_improvements_for_formulation: Make explicit that the calibrated effect-size score s_cal is α-dependent and not monotone-invariant, while the p-value is — this asymmetry can confuse interpretation across figures.
Provide an operational definition of 'convergence' in the Aristotelian Representation Hypothesis (e.g., slope of calibrated mKNN with capacity or limit behavior); the current phrasing is qualitative.
Clarify the choice of s_max for each metric used in figures, especially for unbounded ones, and how cross-metric comparisons of calibrated scores should be interpreted.
State upfront in the main text that Huh et al.'s headline result already used a local metric (mKNN), to clarify what is new versus reframed.

correct: Proposition 4.1: derivation E_H0[||C̃||_F^2] = d_x d_y/(n−1) is correct given the stated i.i.d. assumptions.
Proposition 4.2 / Theorem D.9: mKNN null baseline k/(n−1) follows correctly from uniform distribution of k-NN sets under i.i.d. data with distinct distances.
Proposition D.6: sub-Gaussian maximal inequality E[T_max] ≤ μ + Cσ√log M correctly derived via union bound and tail integration; explicit constant C=3 derivation is sound.
Lemma D.2 and Corollary 5.1: super-uniformity of permutation p-values is a standard randomization-inference result, correctly applied.
Proposition D.3: monotone invariance of rank-based statistics under strictly increasing transformations is standard and correctly stated.
partially_correct: The informal scaling argument that CKA's null baseline is O(d/n) because the denominator scales as O(√d): the proposition only bounds the numerator energy; fluctuations and concentration of the normalizing terms ||Σ_XX||_F · ||Σ_YY||_F are not formally analyzed, leaving the final O(d/n) baseline argument heuristic.

Theorem D.9 assumes pairwise distances are almost surely distinct under absolutely continuous distributions; for finite-precision computations and structured representation distributions (low intrinsic dimension), the tie-breaking assumption is glossed over.

Sub-Gaussian assumption (D.5) is satisfied by bounded metrics via Hoeffding, but σ ≤ (s_max − s_min)/2 is loose; the bound is correct but the effective constant for real similarity matrices is not characterized.
suggested_improvements_for_theoretical_s: Provide a complete derivation of the null CKA baseline including denominator concentration, perhaps invoking standard results for Wishart matrices.
Discuss when the i.i.d. assumption fails (e.g., low intrinsic dimension representations) and how this affects mKNN null baseline.
Quantify the effective number of comparisons for correlated layer pairs (e.g., via mixing or block-correlation arguments) since union bound is conservative.
Add formal analysis showing whether and when calibrated unbiased CKA differs from raw unbiased CKA, given that unbiased HSIC already targets the bias.

supported: Calibration eliminates width-induced null inflation across metrics and noise distributions (Figures 3, 8, 14, 15).
Type-I error control at α=0.05 and increasing power with signal strength (Figure 4).
Aggregation-aware calibration removes depth-driven inflation in controlled synthetic experiments (Figure 5).
Empirical permutation calibration recovers Murphy et al.'s analytical debiased CKA on synthetic data (Figure 12).
Calibrated CKA shows no convergence trend with model capacity, while calibrated mKNN/cycle-kNN/CKNNA retain scaling trends, across 5 vision families and 3 LM families (Figures 6, 16, 17).
Conclusions robust to permutation budget K≥200 (Figure 13) and significance level α∈{0.01,0.05,0.10} (Figures 23–25).
partially_supported: Video-language extension to VideoMAE: based on only 3 video encoder sizes and 3 LM families; the scaling trend with VideoMAE-Huge is a single data point and bottleneck interpretation is speculative (Figure 7).

The 'global convergence largely disappears' claim: calibrated effect sizes flatten but p-values remain <0.005 for all global metric pairs (Figure 18), so it is the scaling trend not the alignment that vanishes.
The locality 'topological vs metric' distinction: rests on small-σ CKA-RBF showing no calibrated alignment (σ=0.1), but at σ=0.1 raw CKA approaches 1.0 (saturation), confounding interpretation.
unsupported_or_overstated: Strong form of the Aristotelian Representation Hypothesis as 'convergence': calibrated mKNN scores show modest values (0.10–0.20) with gradual upward trends; this does not establish convergence in a strong limiting sense.

Claim that cross-modal representations 'agree on which points are neighbors but not on how close they are': based primarily on a single hyperparameter choice (σ=0.1) without normalization analysis or alternative tests (e.g., local distance rank correlation).

Claim that 'global spectral convergence is largely confounded by width and depth': partially over-stated since unbiased CKA (which corrects width bias analytically) is not shown on the actual PRH data to confirm the source of confounding.
suggested_improvements_for_claim_support: Add a direct comparison of calibrated CKA against unbiased CKA (Song et al.) and debiased CKA (Murphy et al.) on the actual PRH model pairs to confirm the disappearance of scaling is robust to method.
Quantify the scaling trend (e.g., regression slope of calibrated mKNN vs LM capability) with confidence intervals to substantiate 'retains alignment' more rigorously.
Include random/untrained model baselines and within-modality (vision-vision, language-language) comparisons as sanity checks.
Test the topological-vs-metric claim with normalized kernels (e.g., median-heuristic bandwidth) and complementary measures like local Procrustes or local distance rank correlation.
Replicate with larger n to demonstrate that conclusions are not artifacts of n=1024 in a regime where d/n is large.

experimental_rigor.baseline_quality: Adequate for the synthetic settings; for PRH, calibrated scores are compared against raw scores but not against alternative debiasing methods (unbiased CKA, Murphy et al.'s debiased CKA, Chun et al.'s dep-cols CKA) on the actual model data — only on synthetic data.
missing_baselines: Untrained/random model controls on PRH to confirm calibrated alignment is zero in the absence of training.
Within-modality (vision-vision, language-language) comparisons to disentangle cross-modal phenomenon from general representation similarity.
Direct comparison of calibrated CKA to Murphy et al.'s and Chun et al.'s debiased CKA on real PRH model pairs.
Comparison to existing permutation-test approaches in representational similarity (Ding et al. 2021).
experimental_rigor.dataset_quality: Reasonable for reproducing Huh et al.'s protocol (WIT, n=1024); sample size is small relative to embedding dimensions, leaving the framework in a high-d/n regime. PVD test set for video uses 1024 samples.
better_datasets: Larger samples from WIT or other paired image-text datasets to test sample-size robustness.
Multiple datasets (e.g., COCO, Conceptual Captions) to verify generality of the local vs global pattern.
Additional modalities (audio-text, code-text) to test the modality-agnostic strength of the Aristotelian hypothesis.
experimental_rigor.metric_appropriateness: Broad set of metrics covered (CKA linear/RBF/unbiased, mKNN, cycle-kNN, CKNNA, RSA, CCA/SVCCA/PWCCA, RV, Procrustes), appropriate for the metric-agnostic framing.
better_metric: Local distance rank correlation as an additional test of metric (not just topological) agreement.
Procrustes on local neighborhoods as a complementary test of local metric structure.
missing_ablations: Choice of distance for k-NN (Euclidean vs cosine) on mKNN conclusions.
Effect of feature normalization on CKA-RBF bandwidth interpretation.
Stability of aggregation-aware calibration with small K (only scalar case is analyzed).
Comparison of aggregation operators (mean, top-k mean) beyond max.
experimental_rigor.statistical_significance: Permutation p-values reported with Benjamini-Hochberg correction; super-uniformity formally established. However, confidence intervals on calibrated effect sizes (e.g., across random seeds or bootstrap on n) are not reported in main figures.
better_significance_test_or_confidence_i: Add bootstrap confidence intervals on calibrated scores for PRH plots.
Report regression slopes with confidence intervals to quantify scaling trends formally.
Use restricted permutations to test robustness under potential WIT topic-clustering structure.
experimental_rigor.hyperparameter_clarity: K=200 permutations, α=0.05, k=10 for neighborhood metrics are clearly stated; RBF bandwidth values (0.1, 0.5, 2.0, 5.0) are stated but not justified relative to feature scales.
possible_confounders: Within-family layer correlations make the effective number of comparisons smaller than M=L_A·L_B, possibly under-stating depth confounding (or over-stating it depending on framing).
n=1024 places experiments in high-d/n regime where calibration corrections are large; conclusions may differ with larger n.
Feature norms differ across models, affecting RBF kernel bandwidth interpretation in F.9.
WIT samples may have topic/group clustering violating strict exchangeability.
Modality choice: image-text vs video-text with limited number of video encoders limits generalizability.
confounder_reduction_alternatives: Use restricted/block permutations to address potential clustering in WIT.
Repeat PRH analysis at multiple sample sizes (e.g., 256, 1024, 4096) to verify conclusions are not n-dependent.
Normalize features before applying RBF kernels and use median-heuristic bandwidths in F.9.
Include more video encoder families (V-JEPA, InternVideo) to strengthen video-language findings.
suggestions_for_improving_reproducibilit: Specify exact embedding extraction (layer indices, pooling) for each model family.
Provide random seeds used for permutation sampling and the WIT subsampling.
Document RBF bandwidth selection criterion and feature normalization choices.
Release computed similarity matrices and null distributions alongside code to enable verification.

strengths: Identifies and clearly characterizes two real, important confounders (width-driven O(d/n) null baselines and depth-driven look-elsewhere inflation) that undermine cross-scale representational similarity comparisons.

Proposes a unified, metric-agnostic permutation-based calibration framework with finite-sample super-uniformity guarantees (Lemma D.2, Corollary 5.1) and an aggregation-aware extension that correctly addresses the maxT-style inflation at the level of the reported statistic.
Theoretical results (Propositions 4.1, 4.2, D.6, D.9) are correct, clearly stated, and directly motivate the empirical methodology.

Empirical validation is thorough: controlled synthetic Type-I/power experiments across multiple metric families and noise distributions, agreement with Murphy et al.'s analytical debiased CKA, and large-scale PRH re-evaluation across 204 vision-language pairs and a video-language extension.

Robustness ablations across α, K, neighborhood size k, and RBF bandwidth strengthen the central qualitative finding that global spectral scaling vanishes after calibration while local neighborhood scaling persists.
Provides clean pseudocode (Algorithms 1, 2) and released code, lowering adoption barriers; likely to become a community-standard practice for similarity-based comparisons.
The reframing into the Aristotelian Representation Hypothesis (topological rather than metric agreement) is a substantively meaningful and defensible refinement of an influential prior claim.
weaknesses: On the actual PRH data, calibrated CKA is not directly compared against existing analytical bias corrections (Murphy et al., Chun et al., or Song et al.'s unbiased CKA); the agreement is shown only on synthetic data, leaving open whether the disappearance of global convergence is specific to the permutation null.

The strong-form 'convergence' language in the Aristotelian Representation Hypothesis is not operationally defined; calibrated mKNN values remain modest (~0.10-0.20) with gradual upward trends, evidence consistent with persistent alignment but not strong-sense convergence to a shared limit.

The 'topological vs metric' interpretation rests largely on small-bandwidth (σ=0.1) CKA-RBF showing no calibrated alignment, but at σ=0.1 raw scores saturate near 1.0, confounding the interpretation; no normalization analysis or alternative test (e.g., local distance rank correlation, local Procrustes) is provided.

Missing baselines on PRH: no untrained/random-model controls, no within-modality (vision-vision, language-language) controls, and no direct comparison with Ding et al. (2021)'s permutation-based similarity testing.

Confidence intervals/bootstrap variability on calibrated scores and quantitative slopes for the 'scaling trend' are not reported in main figures, making claims like 'retains scaling' versus 'flattens' somewhat qualitative.
Sample size n=1024 places experiments in a high d/n regime where calibration corrections are largest; robustness to larger n on actual model data is not demonstrated.
Video-language extension relies on a single encoder family (VideoMAE, three sizes), and the 'bottleneck' interpretation of smaller encoders is speculative.

The novelty is primarily a synthesis of standard randomization-inference tools (permutation tests, maxT correction) adapted to representational similarity; while the application and unification are valuable, the underlying statistical machinery is classical.
minor_issues: The informal claim that CKA's null baseline scales as O(d/n) glosses over concentration of the denominator (||Σ_XX||_F · ||Σ_YY||_F); only the numerator energy is formally derived.
The calibrated score s_cal is α-dependent and not monotone-invariant while p-values are; this asymmetry deserves more explicit discussion to avoid misinterpretation of figures.
RBF bandwidths in the locality analysis (σ ∈ {0.1, 0.5, 2.0, 5.0}) are not justified relative to feature scales; median-heuristic comparison would clarify.
Exchangeability under H0 (Assumption 3.1) may be mildly violated by topic/group structure in WIT; restricted permutations are mentioned in the limitations but not empirically tested.
Choice of distance for k-NN (Euclidean vs cosine) and effect of feature normalization on mKNN/CKA-RBF conclusions are not ablated.
Some figures would benefit from explicit reporting of regression slopes with confidence intervals for the scaling trends.
The framing implicitly acknowledges but could state more prominently that Huh et al.'s headline mKNN result is preserved (and only their global-CKA claim is overturned).
Typo/citation oddity: 'Aristotle, ca. 350 B.C.E' citation is stylistic but slightly awkward as a formal reference.
questions_for_authors: On the actual PRH data, how does calibrated CKA compare to Murphy et al.'s debiased CKA, Chun et al.'s dep-cols CKA, and Song et al.'s unbiased CKA? Does the disappearance of global scaling reproduce under these analytical corrections?
Can you provide quantitative slopes (with confidence intervals) of calibrated mKNN vs. language model capability across families to substantiate the 'scaling trend persists' claim?
What do calibrated scores look like for untrained/randomly initialized vision or language models as baseline controls?

How sensitive are the locality conclusions (Section F.9) to feature normalization and median-heuristic RBF bandwidth selection? Could the σ=0.1 result reflect kernel saturation rather than absence of metric agreement?
Does the local-vs-global pattern persist at larger n (e.g., 4096, 8192), where d/n is smaller and width confounding is intrinsically milder?
Have you tested restricted/block permutations to address possible topic clustering in WIT, and do results change?
Could you complement the topological-vs-metric claim with a direct test such as local distance rank correlation or local Procrustes within k-neighborhoods?
How would you formally define 'convergence' in the Aristotelian Representation Hypothesis (e.g., a limit object, a slope threshold, or a fixed-point characterization)?
parsed.recommendation_rationale: The paper identifies real, well-characterized confounders in representational similarity comparisons and provides a principled, metric-agnostic permutation-based calibration framework with sound theoretical guarantees and broad empirical validation. The re-evaluation of the Platonic Representation Hypothesis is thorough and yields a meaningful conceptual refinement (the Aristotelian Representation Hypothesis), supported by consistent evidence across model families, modalities, metrics, and design choices. The methodological contributions are largely a careful synthesis of classical randomization-inference tools rather than fundamentally new statistical theory, and several interpretive claims—particularly 'convergence' language and the topological-vs-metric distinction—are somewhat over-stated and would benefit from additional baselines (untrained models, within-modality controls), direct comparisons with existing analytical debiasing methods on the actual PRH data, and quantitative slope analyses. Nevertheless, the work is technically sound, clearly written, well-engineered, and likely to influence reporting practices in representation similarity and PRH-style scaling studies. These concerns are addressable with revisions rather than fatal.

parsed.summary_of_paper:
The paper identifies two systematic confounders that distort representational similarity comparisons across neural networks: (i) a width-driven non-zero null baseline for spectral metrics like CKA that scales as O(d/n), and (ii) a depth-driven multiple-comparisons inflation when summarizing layer-pair similarities via max/top-k operators. The authors derive these effects theoretically (Propositions 4.1, 4.2, and a sub-Gaussian maximal inequality for the depth confounder) and propose a unified, metric-agnostic permutation-based null-calibration framework. The framework transforms any raw similarity into a bounded calibrated effect size with finite-sample super-uniform p-values, and an aggregation-aware variant correctly calibrates the entire reported summary statistic by applying consistent permutations across layers.

The authors then revisit the Platonic Representation Hypothesis (PRH) using their calibration. Across 204 vision–language model pairs (and a video–language extension), the global spectral convergence trend in raw CKA largely vanishes after calibration, whereas local neighborhood metrics (mKNN, cycle-kNN, CKNNA) retain a robust scaling trend. A locality analysis (varying mKNN's k and CKA-RBF's bandwidth) suggests that the persistent alignment is topological (neighbor identity) rather than metric (exact local distances), motivating a refined claim: the Aristotelian Representation Hypothesis, that networks converge to shared local neighborhood relationships.
main_strengths: Identifies and clearly characterizes two real and important confounders (width-driven O(d/n) null baselines and depth-driven look-elsewhere inflation) that undermine cross-scale representational similarity comparisons.

Provides a unified, metric-agnostic permutation calibration framework with finite-sample super-uniformity guarantees (Lemma D.2, Corollary 5.1) and an aggregation-aware extension that addresses maxT-style inflation at the level of the reported statistic, not the entry-wise level.
The theoretical results (Propositions 4.1, 4.2, D.6, D.9) are correct, clearly stated, and tightly connected to the empirical methodology.

Empirical validation is thorough: controlled synthetic Type-I/power experiments across multiple metric families and noise distributions, agreement with Murphy et al.'s analytical debiased CKA, a large-scale PRH re-evaluation across 204 vision–language pairs, and a video–language extension.

Robustness ablations across α, permutation budget K, neighborhood size k, and RBF bandwidth strengthen the central qualitative finding that global spectral scaling vanishes after calibration while local neighborhood scaling persists.
Clean pseudocode (Algorithms 1, 2) and released code lower adoption barriers; the framework is practically usable and likely to influence reporting practices in similarity-based comparison studies.
The reframing into the Aristotelian Representation Hypothesis (topological rather than metric agreement) is a substantively meaningful and defensible refinement of an influential prior claim.
main_weaknesses: On the actual PRH data, calibrated CKA is not directly compared against existing analytical bias corrections (Murphy et al., Chun et al., Song et al.'s unbiased CKA). The agreement is shown only on synthetic data, leaving open whether the disappearance of global convergence is specific to the permutation null or holds under analytical debiasing.

The strong-form 'convergence' language in the Aristotelian Representation Hypothesis is not operationally defined. Calibrated mKNN values remain modest (~0.10–0.20) with gradual upward trends; this is evidence consistent with persistent alignment but not strong-sense convergence to a shared limit.

The 'topological vs metric' interpretation rests largely on small-bandwidth (σ=0.1) CKA-RBF showing no calibrated alignment, but at σ=0.1 raw scores saturate near 1.0, which confounds the interpretation. No normalization analysis or alternative direct test (e.g., local distance rank correlation, local Procrustes within k-neighborhoods) is provided.

Missing baselines on PRH: no untrained/randomly-initialized model controls, no within-modality (vision–vision, language–language) controls, and no direct comparison with Ding et al. (2021)'s permutation-based similarity testing.

Confidence intervals/bootstrap variability on calibrated scores and quantitative slopes for the 'scaling trend persists' versus 'flattens' claims are not reported in main figures, leaving these claims somewhat qualitative.
Sample size n=1024 places PRH experiments in a high d/n regime where calibration corrections are largest. Robustness to larger n on actual model data is not demonstrated.
The video–language extension relies on a single encoder family (VideoMAE, three sizes); the 'bottleneck' interpretation of smaller encoders is speculative without further encoder families.

Methodologically, the contribution is primarily a careful synthesis of standard randomization-inference tools (permutation tests, maxT correction) adapted to representational similarity; the underlying statistical machinery is classical, even though the application and unification are valuable.
detailed_comments: Section 4.1: The informal claim that linear CKA's null baseline scales as O(d/n) glosses over the behavior of the denominator (||Σ_XX||_F · ||Σ_YY||_F). Only the numerator energy ||C̃||_F^2 is formally derived. A short calculation or appeal to standard concentration results for the denominator would tighten the argument.

Section 5.1: The asymmetry between calibrated scores (α-dependent, not monotone-invariant) and p-values (rank-based, monotone-invariant) is acknowledged briefly but deserves more emphasis. Readers comparing the calibrated effect-size plots across metrics could be misled if they do not internalize this distinction.

Section 6.3 and Figures 6, 7: The qualitative judgments 'no systematic increase with scale' (global) versus 'retains alignment trend' (local) would be much more convincing with regression slopes and confidence intervals or a formal scaling-trend test. Without this, the visual differences are real but their magnitude is hard to assess.

Section F.9 (locality analysis): The choice of RBF bandwidths σ ∈ {0.1, 0.5, 2.0, 5.0} is not justified relative to the feature scales of the underlying representations. Reporting median-heuristic bandwidths or feature norm statistics would clarify whether σ=0.1 reflects an extremely under-smoothed kernel rather than a 'truly local' geometric probe. As written, the 'topological vs metric' interpretation is suggestive but not airtight.

Aggregation-aware calibration: The illustration that naive entry-wise calibration still inflates the max (Figure 5) is a particularly useful and clarifying result; this should perhaps be highlighted more prominently in the main text as it directly contradicts a natural practitioner instinct.

Section 6.3: Huh et al.'s headline mKNN scaling result is essentially preserved after calibration; only the global CKA claim is overturned. This nuance should be stated more prominently to give an accurate accounting of what the present work refines versus refutes.

Section A (Limitations): The mention of restricted permutations under non-exchangeable structure (e.g., topic clusters in WIT) is appropriate but is not empirically tested. WIT in particular has well-known topic structure, and a small ablation with block-restricted permutations would meaningfully strengthen Section 6.3.

Choice of distance for mKNN (Euclidean versus cosine), as well as feature normalization choices, can materially affect both spectral and neighborhood metrics. An ablation would address an obvious practitioner concern.
The Aristotle reference is stylistically charming but the in-text citation 'Aristotle, ca. 350 B.C.E' is slightly awkward as a formal bibliographic reference; consider standardizing.

Figure 2(a) is somewhat dense; the labeling of x/y axes and the relationship between subpanels could be clarified in the caption to make the visual claim about width inflation more legible at a glance.
questions_for_authors: On the actual PRH data, how does calibrated CKA compare to Murphy et al.'s debiased CKA, Chun et al.'s dep-cols CKA, and Song et al.'s unbiased CKA? Does the disappearance of global scaling reproduce under these analytical corrections, or is it specific to the permutation null?

Can you provide quantitative slopes (with confidence intervals) of calibrated mKNN versus language model capability for each vision family, to substantiate the 'scaling trend persists' claim quantitatively rather than visually?
What do calibrated CKA and mKNN look like for untrained or randomly initialized vision/language models as baseline controls, and for within-modality comparisons (vision–vision, language–language)?

How sensitive are the locality conclusions (Section F.9) to feature normalization and to median-heuristic RBF bandwidth selection? Could the σ=0.1 result reflect kernel saturation rather than absence of metric agreement?

Does the local-vs-global pattern persist at substantially larger n (e.g., 4096, 8192), where d/n is smaller and width confounding is intrinsically milder? This would help distinguish 'calibration removes spurious convergence' from 'small-n regime artifact'.
Have you tested restricted/block permutations to address possible topic clustering in WIT? Do results change materially?
Could you complement the topological-vs-metric claim with a direct test such as local distance rank correlation or local Procrustes alignment within k-neighborhoods?

How would you formally define 'convergence' in the Aristotelian Representation Hypothesis (e.g., a limit object, a slope threshold for scaling, or a fixed-point characterization)? Without such a definition, the hypothesis risks remaining qualitative.
For the video–language extension, would additional video encoder families (e.g., VideoSwin, V-JEPA) corroborate the 'smaller encoders act as a bottleneck' interpretation?
suggestions_for_improvement: Add a direct comparison on the actual PRH data between calibrated CKA and existing analytical debiasing methods (Murphy et al., Chun et al., unbiased CKA). This would substantially strengthen the claim that global convergence is a confounding artifact rather than a permutation-specific phenomenon.
Include untrained-model and within-modality baselines on the WIT/PRH setup to anchor the magnitude of calibrated scores.

Report regression slopes with bootstrap confidence intervals for calibrated alignment versus model capability, and add a formal scaling-trend test (e.g., null of zero slope) to make the local-vs-global contrast quantitative.

Strengthen the topological-vs-metric distinction by adding a direct probe such as local distance rank correlation (Spearman of distances within k-neighborhoods) or local Procrustes, instead of relying solely on small-σ CKA-RBF behavior.
Justify RBF bandwidth choices via median heuristic or feature-norm statistics, and ablate over feature normalization and distance metric (Euclidean vs cosine) for mKNN.

Operationalize 'convergence' in the Aristotelian Representation Hypothesis (e.g., a specified scaling-law form, a limiting object, or an explicit non-trivial slope criterion) so the hypothesis becomes empirically falsifiable.
Test restricted/block permutations on WIT to address possible non-exchangeability from topic clustering.
Repeat the PRH experiment at larger n (where computationally feasible) to demonstrate robustness outside the high-d/n regime.

More clearly emphasize in the main text that Huh et al.'s mKNN-based convergence finding is largely preserved after calibration, so that readers do not misread the contribution as overturning PRH wholesale.

Add a brief subsection in the main text discussing the α-dependence and non-monotone-invariance of the calibrated score s_cal, alongside the monotone-invariance of p-values, to prevent misinterpretation.
parsed.overall_recommendation: Accept (with revisions). The paper makes a principled, technically sound, and broadly useful methodological contribution and applies it to a high-profile open question with substantive findings. Several interpretive claims would be strengthened by additional baselines and quantitative analyses, but no concern rises to the level of a fatal flaw.

misrepresentations: The draft states that Huh et al.'s mKNN-based convergence finding is 'essentially preserved' / 'largely preserved' after calibration. This is broadly correct but slightly overstates the nuance: the paper shows local neighborhood alignment persists after calibration, but does not explicitly verify that Huh et al.'s specific mKNN scaling claim quantitatively survives — the trend is preserved qualitatively in some families (DINOv2, CLIP) but flatter in others (MAE, INet21K).
missed_strengths: The paper makes a clear conceptual distinction between rank-based p-values (monotone-invariant) and effect-size calibrated scores (α-dependent by design), which is a genuinely thoughtful design choice worth crediting.

Table 1 in Appendix B provides a clean positioning relative to prior debiasing work, clarifying that prior methods are metric-specific while this framework is metric-agnostic and also handles aggregation — a strength the draft only partially credits.
missed_weaknesses: Proposition 4.1 only derives the expected null energy E[||C̃||_F^2], not the expectation of CKA itself; the O(d/n) baseline claim for CKA is informal because CKA is a ratio of random quantities and E[ratio] ≠ ratio of expectations. The draft mentions this in detailed comments but could elevate it to a more substantive soundness concern.

The sub-Gaussian uniform tail assumption (Assumption D.5) is stated as covering 'common bounded metrics' via Hoeffding, but the constant σ ≤ (s_max − s_min)/2 is loose and may not reflect realistic tail behavior of CKA across correlated layer pairs; the resulting √log M bound is therefore qualitative rather than tight.
too_strong_claims: Calling the methodological contribution 'primarily a careful synthesis of standard randomization-inference tools' may understate the novelty of the aggregation-aware variant in this specific application context; consider softening.

The claim that 'no concern rises to the level of a fatal flaw' is appropriate but should be paired with explicit acknowledgement that the central interpretive claim (topological vs metric) rests on one ablation and would benefit from corroborating evidence.
non_actionable_criticisms: The comment about the Aristotle citation being 'stylistically charming' but 'awkward' is minor and borderline non-actionable; either drop or convert to a concrete suggestion (e.g., use a standard edition citation).
corrections_needed: Soften the 'largely preserved' framing of Huh et al.'s mKNN result to acknowledge that the trend persists qualitatively but with family-dependent strength.
Add explicit weakness noting that Proposition 4.1 derives null energy of the cross-covariance, not of CKA directly, so the O(d/n) baseline statement for CKA is informal (ratio of random quantities).
Acknowledge as a strength the explicit design rationale for using a non-monotone-invariant effect size alongside monotone-invariant p-values.
Credit Table 1 / Appendix B for the explicit positioning relative to prior metric-specific debiasing work.
Drop or rephrase the Aristotle citation stylistic comment to be either concrete or removed.
Soften the characterization of the contribution as 'primarily a synthesis' to avoid undervaluing the aggregation-aware extension.

final_review.summary_of_paper:
The paper identifies two systematic confounders that distort representational similarity comparisons across neural networks: (i) a width-driven non-zero null baseline for spectral metrics like CKA that scales as O(d/n), and (ii) a depth-driven multiple-comparisons inflation when summarizing layer-pair similarities via max/top-k operators. The authors derive these effects theoretically (Propositions 4.1, 4.2, and a sub-Gaussian maximal inequality for the depth confounder) and propose a unified, metric-agnostic permutation-based null-calibration framework. The framework transforms any raw similarity into a bounded calibrated effect size with finite-sample super-uniform p-values, and an aggregation-aware variant calibrates the entire reported summary statistic by applying consistent permutations across layers.

The authors then revisit the Platonic Representation Hypothesis (PRH) using their calibration. Across 204 vision–language model pairs (plus a video–language extension), the global spectral convergence trend in raw CKA largely vanishes after calibration, whereas local neighborhood metrics (mKNN, cycle-kNN, CKNNA) retain a scaling trend (with family-dependent strength). A locality analysis (varying mKNN's k and CKA-RBF's bandwidth) suggests that the persistent alignment is topological (neighbor identity) rather than metric (exact local distances), motivating a refined claim: the Aristotelian Representation Hypothesis, that networks converge to shared local neighborhood relationships.
main_strengths: Identifies and clearly characterizes two real and important confounders (width-driven O(d/n) null baselines and depth-driven look-elsewhere inflation) that undermine cross-scale representational similarity comparisons.

Provides a unified, metric-agnostic permutation calibration framework with finite-sample super-uniformity guarantees (Lemma D.2, Corollary 5.1), and a non-trivial aggregation-aware extension that calibrates the entire reported max/top-k statistic via consistent layer-wise permutations rather than per-entry calibration.
Theoretical results (Propositions 4.1, 4.2, D.6, D.9) are correct, clearly stated, and tightly connected to the empirical methodology.

Thoughtful design distinction between rank-based, monotone-invariant permutation p-values and an explicitly non-monotone-invariant calibrated effect size, with a clear rationale for why a rank-invariant calibration cannot correct scale-dependent baselines.

Table 1 (Appendix B) provides a clean positioning against prior debiasing approaches (Murphy et al., Chun et al., Cui et al., Diedrichsen et al., Cai et al., Smilde et al.), making explicit that prior corrections are metric-specific while this framework is metric-agnostic and additionally handles selection inflation.

Empirical validation is thorough: controlled synthetic Type-I/power experiments across multiple metric families and noise distributions, agreement with Murphy et al.'s analytical debiased CKA on synthetic data, a large-scale PRH re-evaluation across 204 vision–language pairs, and a video–language extension.

Robustness ablations across α, permutation budget K, neighborhood size k, and RBF bandwidth strengthen the central qualitative finding that global spectral scaling vanishes after calibration while local neighborhood scaling persists.
Clean pseudocode (Algorithms 1, 2) and released code lower adoption barriers; the framework is practically usable and likely to influence reporting practices.
The reframing into the Aristotelian Representation Hypothesis (topological rather than metric agreement) is a substantively meaningful refinement of an influential prior claim.
main_weaknesses: On the actual PRH data, calibrated CKA is not directly compared against existing analytical bias corrections (Murphy et al., Chun et al., Song et al.'s unbiased CKA). The agreement is shown only on synthetic data, leaving open whether the disappearance of global convergence is specific to the permutation null or also holds under analytical debiasing.

Proposition 4.1 derives the expected null Frobenius energy E[||C̃||_F^2] of the cross-covariance, but the O(d/n) baseline statement for CKA itself is informal: CKA is a ratio of random quantities and E[ratio] ≠ ratio of expectations, so the headline scaling claim for CKA is justified heuristically rather than proved.

Assumption D.5's sub-Gaussian parameter σ ≤ (s_max−s_min)/2 from Hoeffding is loose and likely does not reflect realistic right-tail behavior of CKA across correlated layer pairs, so the √log M depth inflation bound is qualitatively informative but not tight.

The strong-form 'convergence' language in the Aristotelian Representation Hypothesis is not operationally defined. Calibrated mKNN values remain modest (~0.10–0.20) with gradual upward trends consistent with persistent alignment but not strong-sense convergence to a shared limit.

The 'topological vs metric' interpretation rests largely on small-bandwidth (σ=0.1) CKA-RBF showing no calibrated alignment, but at σ=0.1 raw scores saturate near 1.0, which complicates the interpretation. No direct test (e.g., local distance rank correlation or local Procrustes within k-neighborhoods) is provided.

Missing baselines on PRH: no untrained/randomly-initialized model controls, no within-modality (vision–vision, language–language) controls, and no direct comparison with Ding et al. (2021)'s permutation-based similarity testing.
Confidence intervals/bootstrap variability on calibrated scores and quantitative slopes for the 'scaling trend persists' versus 'flattens' claims are not reported, leaving these claims qualitative.
Sample size n=1024 places PRH experiments in a high-d/n regime where calibration corrections are largest. Robustness to larger n on actual model data is not demonstrated.
The video–language extension relies on a single encoder family (VideoMAE, three sizes); the 'bottleneck' interpretation of smaller encoders is speculative without further encoder families.
detailed_comments: Section 4.1: The informal claim that linear CKA's null baseline scales as O(d/n) glosses over (i) the behavior of the denominator (||Σ_XX||_F · ||Σ_YY||_F) and (ii) the fact that CKA is a ratio of random quantities, so the energy calculation does not directly yield E[CKA] under H_0. A short additional argument (e.g., concentration of the denominator) would tighten the claim.

Section 5.1: The asymmetry between calibrated scores (α-dependent, not monotone-invariant) and p-values (rank-based, monotone-invariant) is acknowledged briefly but deserves more emphasis to prevent readers from misinterpreting effect-size plots across metrics.

Section 6.3 and Figures 6, 7: The qualitative judgments 'no systematic increase with scale' (global) versus 'retains alignment trend' (local) would be more convincing with regression slopes, confidence intervals, or a formal scaling-trend test. Note that the local trend is clearer for some vision families (DINOv2, CLIP) than for others (MAE, INet21K).

Section F.9 (locality analysis): The RBF bandwidths σ ∈ {0.1, 0.5, 2.0, 5.0} are not justified relative to the feature scales of the underlying representations. Reporting median-heuristic bandwidths or feature norm statistics would clarify whether σ=0.1 reflects an extremely under-smoothed kernel rather than a 'truly local' geometric probe.

Aggregation-aware calibration: The illustration that naive entry-wise calibration still inflates the max (Figure 5) is a particularly clarifying result and could be highlighted more prominently as it directly contradicts a natural practitioner instinct.

Section 6.3: The framing should make clear that Huh et al.'s local mKNN scaling result is qualitatively preserved after calibration (with family-dependent strength), while their global CKA-based convergence claim is the one overturned. This nuance is important for an accurate accounting of what the work refines versus refutes.

Section A (Limitations): The mention of restricted permutations under non-exchangeable structure (e.g., topic clusters in WIT) is appropriate but not empirically tested; a block-restricted permutation ablation would strengthen Section 6.3.
Choice of distance for mKNN (Euclidean vs cosine) and feature normalization can materially affect both spectral and neighborhood metrics; an ablation would address an obvious practitioner concern.
Figure 2(a) is somewhat dense; axis labels and the relationship between subpanels could be clarified to make the visual claim about width inflation more legible.
questions_for_authors: On the actual PRH data, how does calibrated CKA compare to Murphy et al.'s debiased CKA, Chun et al.'s dep-cols CKA, and Song et al.'s unbiased CKA? Does the disappearance of global scaling reproduce under these analytical corrections, or is it specific to the permutation null?

Can you provide quantitative slopes (with confidence intervals) of calibrated mKNN versus language model capability for each vision family, to substantiate the 'scaling trend persists' claim quantitatively, especially given the apparent family dependence?
Beyond the energy calculation in Proposition 4.1, can you provide a direct argument (or empirical check on real model representations) that the null expectation of CKA itself scales as O(d/n)?
What do calibrated CKA and mKNN look like for untrained or randomly initialized vision/language models as baseline controls, and for within-modality comparisons (vision–vision, language–language)?

How sensitive are the locality conclusions (Section F.9) to feature normalization and to median-heuristic RBF bandwidth selection? Could the σ=0.1 result reflect kernel saturation rather than absence of metric agreement?
Does the local-vs-global pattern persist at substantially larger n (e.g., 4096, 8192), where d/n is smaller and width confounding is intrinsically milder?
Have you tested restricted/block permutations to address possible topic clustering in WIT? Do results change materially?
Could you complement the topological-vs-metric claim with a direct test such as local distance rank correlation or local Procrustes alignment within k-neighborhoods?
How would you formally define 'convergence' in the Aristotelian Representation Hypothesis (e.g., a limit object, a slope threshold for scaling, or a fixed-point characterization)?
For the video–language extension, would additional video encoder families (e.g., VideoSwin, V-JEPA) corroborate the 'smaller encoders act as a bottleneck' interpretation?
suggestions_for_improvement: Add a direct comparison on the actual PRH data between calibrated CKA and existing analytical debiasing methods (Murphy et al., Chun et al., unbiased CKA). This would substantially strengthen the claim that global convergence is a confounding artifact rather than a permutation-specific phenomenon.

Tighten Section 4.1 by either (a) deriving the null expectation of CKA directly or (b) clearly labeling the O(d/n) statement as a heuristic for the numerator energy, with empirical verification on synthetic data of E[CKA] under H_0.
Include untrained-model and within-modality baselines on the WIT/PRH setup to anchor the magnitude of calibrated scores.

Report regression slopes with bootstrap confidence intervals for calibrated alignment versus model capability, and add a formal scaling-trend test (e.g., null of zero slope) to make the local-vs-global contrast quantitative; explicitly acknowledge vision-family-dependent strength of the local trend.

Strengthen the topological-vs-metric distinction by adding a direct probe such as local distance rank correlation (Spearman of distances within k-neighborhoods) or local Procrustes, instead of relying solely on small-σ CKA-RBF behavior.
Justify RBF bandwidth choices via median heuristic or feature-norm statistics, and ablate over feature normalization and distance metric (Euclidean vs cosine) for mKNN.

Operationalize 'convergence' in the Aristotelian Representation Hypothesis (e.g., a specified scaling-law form, a limiting object, or an explicit non-trivial slope criterion) so the hypothesis becomes empirically falsifiable.
Test restricted/block permutations on WIT to address possible non-exchangeability from topic clustering.
Repeat the PRH experiment at larger n where computationally feasible to demonstrate robustness outside the high-d/n regime.

Emphasize more clearly in the main text that Huh et al.'s mKNN-based local convergence finding is qualitatively preserved after calibration, so that readers do not misread the contribution as overturning PRH wholesale.
Add a brief subsection in the main text discussing the α-dependence and non-monotone-invariance of s_cal alongside the monotone-invariance of p-values.
final_review.overall_recommendation: Accept (with revisions). The paper makes a principled, technically sound, and broadly useful methodological contribution and applies it to a high-profile open question with substantive findings. Several interpretive claims—particularly the topological-vs-metric distinction and the strong 'convergence' language—would benefit from additional baselines, direct probes, and quantitative analyses on real data, but no concern rises to the level of a fatal flaw.

author_facing_improvement_checklist: Compare calibrated CKA against Murphy et al., Chun et al., and unbiased CKA on actual PRH (image–text and video–text) data, not only synthetic.

Tighten the theoretical link between the null Frobenius energy (Proposition 4.1) and the null expectation of CKA itself (a ratio of random quantities); either prove a direct statement or label the O(d/n) baseline as heuristic and verify empirically.
Add untrained/randomly-initialized model controls and within-modality (vision–vision, language–language) controls to the PRH experiments.

Report regression slopes with bootstrap confidence intervals for calibrated alignment vs model capability, with a formal test of zero slope, and explicitly discuss the vision-family-dependent strength of the local trend.
Provide a direct test of the topological-vs-metric distinction (e.g., local distance rank correlation, local Procrustes within k-neighborhoods) rather than relying on small-σ CKA-RBF.
Justify RBF bandwidth and distance/normalization choices via median heuristic and feature-norm statistics; ablate Euclidean vs cosine for mKNN.
Operationalize 'convergence' in the Aristotelian Representation Hypothesis with a concrete falsifiable criterion.
Run block-restricted permutations on WIT to address potential non-exchangeability from topic clustering.
Where feasible, demonstrate robustness at larger n to show the local-vs-global pattern is not a small-sample artifact.
Add a main-text emphasis that Huh et al.'s local mKNN scaling claim is qualitatively preserved after calibration (with family-dependent strength), while their global CKA claim is overturned.
Add a clear main-text discussion of α-dependence and non-monotone-invariance of s_cal versus rank-invariance of p-values.
Improve clarity of Figure 2(a) labeling and consider highlighting Figure 5's entry-wise-calibration counterexample more prominently in the main text.
changes_from_draft: Softened the claim that Huh et al.'s mKNN result is 'essentially preserved' to acknowledge that the local trend persists qualitatively but with vision-family-dependent strength.
Added explicit weakness that Proposition 4.1 derives the null energy of the cross-covariance, not of CKA itself, so the O(d/n) baseline statement for CKA is informal (ratio of random quantities).
Added explicit weakness that Assumption D.5's sub-Gaussian parameter from Hoeffding is loose, making the √log M depth bound qualitatively informative rather than tight.
Added as a strength the principled design choice of pairing rank-invariant p-values with an α-dependent, non-monotone-invariant calibrated effect size.
Added as a strength the explicit positioning against prior debiasing methods provided by Table 1 / Appendix B.
Removed the stylistic comment about the Aristotle citation as non-actionable.

Removed the framing of the contribution as 'primarily a synthesis of standard randomization-inference tools' to avoid undervaluing the aggregation-aware extension, and instead credited the aggregation-aware variant as a non-trivial extension.
Added new questions and improvement suggestions targeting the CKA-vs-energy distinction and direct topological-vs-metric probes.

Updated the overall recommendation rationale to explicitly note that key interpretive claims (topological vs metric, strong 'convergence' language) need corroborating evidence, while keeping the accept recommendation.
